items.prompt: A function performs one full scan of n elements followed by another full scan of n elements. What is the tight asymptotic time?

options.text: O(n).
O(n²).
O(log n).

feedback.reason: Sequential work is added: n + n = 2n.
feedback.details: Constant factors are dropped asymptotically, giving a linear bound.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.quadratic: Sequential phases add rather than multiply.

items.prompt: An outer loop runs n times and an inner loop runs n times for every outer iteration. What structure determines the time bound?

options.text: The iteration counts multiply, yielding n·n operations.
The counts add because the loops are written on separate lines.
The variable names determine the bound.
The inner loop is constant because its body is small.

feedback.reason: Nested independent loops multiply their iteration counts.
feedback.details: The executed body count is n², regardless of how short the body is.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sum: The inner loop executes inside every outer iteration.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.names: Names have no effect on execution count.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.constant: A small body still repeats n² times.

items.prompt: A nested loop has inner index j start at i and run to n - 1. Which conclusion is correct?

options.text: The total iterations form a triangular sum and are O(n²).
The total is O(n) because j never restarts at zero.
Any nested loop with changing bounds is O(n³).
The shrinking inner loop makes total work constant.

feedback.reason: The inner counts are n, n - 1, ..., 1.
feedback.details: Their sum is n(n + 1)/2, which is quadratic.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.always_cubic: There are only two loop dimensions.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.constant: The total still grows with n.

items.prompt: A loop repeatedly divides a positive integer n by 2 until it reaches zero. What drives the iteration count?

options.text: The number of times n can be halved, which is O(log n).
Exactly n iterations.
Exactly one iteration.
The division operator makes the loop O(n²).

feedback.reason: After k iterations, the remaining value is approximately n / 2^k.
feedback.details: The loop stops when 2^k reaches n, so k is logarithmic.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.numeric_n: The value shrinks multiplicatively, not by one.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.constant: Inputs larger than one require multiple halvings.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.quadratic: Operator choice does not determine the number of iterations.

items.prompt: Two pointers each move only forward through an array, and neither pointer moves backward. Why can a nested while loop still be O(n)?

options.text: Across the whole run, each pointer advances at most n times.
Nested loops are always linear.
Variables named left and right imply O(n).
The loop body is constant-time, so total time is constant.

feedback.reason: The relevant count is total pointer movement, not syntactic nesting.
feedback.details: If every iteration advances at least one monotonic pointer and each pointer crosses the array once, total iterations are linear.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.nested_never_matters: Nested loops can be quadratic when inner work resets.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.constant_body: The body still executes multiple times.

items.prompt: A loop may break on the first element, but in the worst case it scans all n elements. What is the worst-case time?

options.text: O(n).
O(1) because an early break exists.
O(log n) because the loop can stop early.
O(n²) because there are two outcomes.

feedback.reason: Worst-case time uses the execution path with the most work among valid inputs of size n.
feedback.details: The possibility of an early exit affects best case, not the worst-case upper bound.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.constant: Worst-case analysis uses an input that delays the break.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.logarithmic: Early termination alone does not imply halving.

items.prompt: A function sorts n values and then scans them once. Which term dominates the total time under comparison sorting?

options.text: O(n log n).
O(n) because the final scan is visible in the return path.
O(n² log n) because phases multiply.
O(1) because sorting is a library call.

feedback.reason: The total is O(n log n + n).
feedback.details: The larger n log n term dominates asymptotically.

items.prompt: Why is the statement "the loop variable is named i, so the loop is O(n)" invalid?

options.text: Complexity follows the loop's update rule, bounds, and work, not the variable name.
The name i is reserved for logarithmic loops.
Every loop is O(n) regardless of its header.
Longer variable names increase the iteration count.

feedback.reason: A variable named i may increment by one, double, reset, or depend on another pointer.
feedback.details: The bound must be derived from how many operations execute.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.all_loops_linear: Loops can be constant, logarithmic, quadratic, or other.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.names_affect_cpu: Names do not alter the algorithm's execution model.

items.prompt: Which rules are valid when combining time costs? Select all that apply.

options.text: Sequential phases add their costs.
Independent nested iteration counts multiply.
Lower-order terms and constant factors can be dropped for asymptotic classification.
Variable names determine which term dominates.

feedback.reason: Time derivation follows composition of executed work.
feedback.details: Syntactic labels do not replace counting.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.variable_names: Names do not encode operation counts.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.sequential_add: All phases execute one after another.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.nested_multiply: The inner work repeats for each outer iteration.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.dominant_term: They do not change growth class.

items.prompt: Which loop patterns can be O(n) despite containing nested syntax? Select all that apply.

options.text: An inner pointer advances monotonically and never resets across outer iterations.
Each input element is processed by the inner body at most a constant number of times overall.
The inner loop scans all n elements from the beginning for every outer iteration.
Each item is pushed once and popped once across all iterations.

feedback.reason: Nested syntax does not force quadratic execution.
feedback.details: The decisive question is how often each unit of input work can occur over the full run.

items.prompt: Which facts are needed to derive a recursive time bound? Select all that apply.

options.text: How many recursive subproblems each call creates.
How the input size changes in each child.
How much nonrecursive work one call performs.
Whether the function is named solve or dfs.

feedback.reason: A recurrence summarizes child structure and work per call.
feedback.details: Without all three, the total executed operations cannot be justified.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.subproblem_size: Depth and total nodes depend on shrinkage.

items.prompt: Which analyses correctly distinguish best, average, amortized, and worst case? Select all that apply.

options.text: Worst case considers the maximum work over inputs of a given size.
Best case considers the minimum work over inputs of a given size.
Average case requires an input distribution or probability model.
Amortized analysis is the same as expected time under random inputs.

feedback.reason: Different complexity labels answer different questions.
feedback.details: Amortized analysis belongs to sequence accounting and should not be substituted for probabilistic average case.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.amortized_distribution: Amortized bounds can be deterministic over every operation sequence.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.worst_max: It bounds the most expensive valid execution.

items.prompt: Arrange the process for deriving time complexity from code.

elements.text: Define the input-size variables and cost model.
Count iterations, recursive calls, and work per operation.
Add sequential costs and multiply repeated nested costs where justified.
Keep the dominant asymptotic term and state the case being analyzed.

feedback.reason: A defensible bound begins with explicit inputs and ends with a simplified execution count.
feedback.details: Skipping directly to a familiar loop shape invites heuristic errors.

items.prompt: Arrange the reasoning for a monotonic two-pointer loop.

elements.text: Identify which pointer movements can occur.
Show each pointer advances at most n positions.
Add the total advances across the entire run.
Conclude O(n) iterations when each iteration advances a pointer.

feedback.reason: Aggregate movement, not loop nesting, determines the bound.
feedback.details: A pointer that never retreats cannot cause repeated full rescans.

items.prompt: Arrange the analysis of a divide-and-conquer algorithm with two half-size calls and O(n) merge work.

elements.text: Observe that recursion has O(log n) levels.
Show the total merge work per level is O(n).
Conclude O(n log n) total time.

feedback.reason: The branching doubles while subproblem sizes halve, keeping each level's combined work linear.
feedback.details: Linear work repeated across logarithmically many levels gives n log n.

items.prompt: Arrange the simplification of O(n² + 3n log n + 7n + 500).

elements.text: Compare n², n log n, n, and 1.
Identify n² as the fastest-growing term.

feedback.reason: Asymptotic simplification preserves the term that dominates for large n.
feedback.details: The discarded terms may matter in practice but do not change the growth class.

items.prompt: A loop executes once for each of n elements and performs O(1) work per iteration. Select time complexity.
constraints: The loop always completes all n iterations.

feedback.reason: One constant-time body execution occurs for each of n indices.
feedback.details: The total operation count grows linearly.

items.prompt: An outer loop runs n times. For outer index i, the inner loop runs exactly i times. Select time complexity.

feedback.reason: The body count is 0 + 1 + ... + (n - 1).
feedback.details: That triangular sum is Θ(n²).

items.prompt: A positive variable starts at n and is divided by 2 each iteration until it is below 1. Select time complexity.
constraints: Each division and loop body are O(1).

feedback.reason: Each iteration reduces the magnitude by a constant factor.
feedback.details: The number of halvings before reaching 1 is logarithmic.

items.prompt: For each of n input elements, an algorithm performs one binary search over n sorted values. Select total time complexity.
constraints: Each binary search is O(log n).

feedback.reason: The logarithmic search cost repeats n times.
feedback.details: Multiplying the independent iteration counts gives n log n.

items.prompt: Two monotonic pointers scan an array of length n. Each iteration advances at least one pointer, and neither pointer moves backward. Select time complexity.
constraints: Each pointer advances at most n times.

feedback.reason: The total number of pointer advances is at most 2n.
feedback.details: Nested control flow does not increase the aggregate movement bound.

items.prompt: A divide-and-conquer algorithm creates two subproblems of size n/2 and performs O(n) additional work per call. Select time complexity.
constraints: Assume n is a power of two and the base case is O(1).

feedback.reason: The recursion has logarithmically many levels and O(n) combined work at each level.
feedback.details: The total is O(n log n).
